Source: Sokolis et al., J Biomech 2006;39(15):2808-2816; Pearce & Boughner, Circ Res 1987. Normal range 1.5-2.5 mm.
Notes: Measured by CT/MRI. Increases with age and hypertension.

Source: Lang et al., J Am Soc Echocardiogr 2005;18:1440-1463 (ASE/EAE guidelines). Normal 6-11 mm diastole.
Notes: Measured by M-mode echocardiography. >11 mm = LVH.

Source: Lang et al., J Am Soc Echocardiogr 2005;18:1440-1463. Normal systolic 12-16 mm.
Notes: Wall thickens ~50% during systolic contraction.

Source: Lang et al., J Am Soc Echocardiogr 2005;18:1440-1463. Normal <5 mm; typical 3-4 mm.
Notes: RV is thinner than LV due to lower pressure workload.

Dimension_Description: Left anterior descending artery lumen diameter
Source: Dodge et al., Circulation 1992;86:232-246. LAD proximal 3.6-3.8 mm; RCA 3.4-3.9 mm; LCx 3.0-3.5 mm.
Notes: Measured by quantitative coronary angiography.

Source: Fung YC, Biomechanics: Circulation, 2nd ed, Springer 1997. Range 5-10 um; typical 8 um. Also: Zweifach BW, Annu Rev Physiol 1973.
Notes: 8 um = single RBC must deform to pass through.

Dimension_Description: Wall thickness of large veins (e.g., saphenous)
Source: Rhodin JAG, Architecture of the vessel wall. In: Handbook of Physiology, Bohr et al., 1980. Large veins 0.3-0.7 mm.
Notes: Thinner than arterial walls; less smooth muscle.

Source: Lorenz et al., Circulation 2007;115(4):459-467; Stein et al., JACC 2008. Normal <0.9 mm; mean ~0.6-0.7 mm in middle-aged adults.
Notes: Key cardiovascular risk marker measured by B-mode ultrasound. >0.9 mm = abnormal; >1.5 mm = plaque.

Source: Breatnach et al., AJR Am J Roentgenol 1984;142:903-906. Tracheal wall 2-3 mm by CT; cartilaginous rings ~3 mm.
Notes: C-shaped cartilage rings anterolaterally; membranous wall posteriorly (~1 mm thinner).

Source: McNamara et al., Radiology 1992;182:829-836; Kim et al., J Comput Assist Tomogr 2004. Segmental bronchial wall 1.0-1.5 mm by HRCT.
Notes: Increases in asthma, COPD, chronic bronchitis.

Source: Weibel ER, The Pathway for Oxygen, Harvard Univ Press, 2005. Total barrier 0.2-0.6 um; thinnest ~0.2 um, mean ~0.5 um.
Notes: Composed of alveolar epithelium + fused basement membrane + capillary endothelium. Thinnest blood-gas barrier in body.

Structure: Lung parenchyma depth (chest wall to lung surface)
Dimension_Description: Approximate distance from skin surface through chest wall to visceral pleura
Source: Webb WR, Mueller NL, Naidich DP, HRCT of the Lung, 5th ed, LWW 2015. Chest wall thickness varies 20-40 mm depending on location and body habitus; typical ~25 mm anterolateral.
Notes: Composed of skin + subcutaneous fat + intercostal muscles + parietal pleura. Highly variable with BMI.

Source: Wang NS, Anatomy of the pleura. In: Chretien J et al., The Pleura in Health and Disease, Dekker 1985. Visceral pleura ~0.1-0.4 mm in humans.
Notes: Human visceral pleura is thicker than in most mammals; contains systemic blood supply.

Source: Defined by endoscopic ultrasound (EUS). Puli et al., Gastrointest Endosc 2005. Normal EUS wall thickness 2-4 mm, mean ~3 mm.
Notes: 5 EUS layers visible. >5 mm raises concern for pathology.

Source: Malouf et al., AJR 2000. Normal gastric wall thickness 3-5 mm when distended; up to 7 mm when collapsed. CT measurement.
Notes: Thickest in antrum and fundus due to rugal folds.

Source: Macari & Balthazar, Radiology 2001;221:422-428. Normal small bowel wall <3 mm when distended; typical ~1.5-2.0 mm.
Notes: Measured by CT enterography. >3 mm = abnormal.

Source: Macari & Balthazar, Radiology 2001. Normal colonic wall thickness <3 mm when distended. Also: Wittenberg et al., AJR 2002.
Notes: Measured on CT colonography. >3-4 mm = abnormal thickening.

Dimension_Description: Height of jejunal villi from crypt base
Source: Marsh MN, Gastroenterology 1992;102:330-354 (Marsh classification). Jejunal villi 500-1000 um (0.5-1.0 mm); duodenal shorter ~350-500 um.
Notes: Villus atrophy (flattening) is diagnostic of celiac disease. Villus:crypt ratio normally 3-5:1.

Source: Kratzer et al., Ultraschall Med 2003;24:162-167. Normal liver span by ultrasound: mean 14-16 cm in MCL; up to 17 cm in tall males.
Notes: >16 cm at MCL suggests hepatomegaly.

Source: Dodds et al., AJR 1991;157:1205-1210. Head: 25-30 mm; Body: 15-25 mm (mean ~20 mm); Tail: 15-25 mm. Decreases with age.
Notes: Measured on CT. Size decreases with age due to fatty infiltration.

Source: Rumack et al., Diagnostic Ultrasound, 5th ed, Elsevier 2018. Normal fasting GB wall <3 mm; typically 1-2 mm.
Notes: >3 mm suggests acute cholecystitis, cirrhosis, ascites, hepatitis, or postprandial state.

Source: Fischl & Dale, PNAS 2000;97:11050-11055; Zilles K, Cortex 2005. Range 1.5-4.5 mm; mean ~2.5 mm. Primary motor cortex thickest (~4.5 mm), primary visual cortex thinnest (~1.5 mm).
Notes: Measured by MRI FreeSurfer analysis. Thins with aging and neurodegenerative disease.

Dimension_Description: AP thickness of corpus callosum body on midsagittal
Source: Bermudez & Bhatt, Brain 2014. Witelson 1989. Genu ~10 mm; Body ~6-7 mm; Splenium ~10 mm. Total length 70-80 mm.
Notes: Largest white matter commissure. Measured on midsagittal MRI.

Source: Sherman et al., AJNR 1990;11:659-664; Kameyama et al., AJNR 1994. Cervical cord: transverse 13-14 mm, AP 7-8 mm. Thoracic: ~10 mm transverse.
Notes: Measured on axial MRI. Cervical enlargement (C4-T1) is widest segment.

Structure: Peripheral nerve diameter (median nerve at wrist)
Source: Buchberger et al., Radiology 1992. Median nerve CSA at wrist ~8-10 mm2 (diameter ~2-3 mm). >13 mm2 suggests carpal tunnel syndrome.
Notes: Measured by high-resolution ultrasound.

Dimension_Description: Thickness of myelin wrapping around axon
Source: Waxman SG, Determinants of conduction velocity, Muscle Nerve 1980;3:141-150; Hildebrand et al., J Neurosci 1993. Myelin thickness ~1-4 um depending on fiber diameter; optimal g-ratio ~0.6-0.7.
Notes: Myelin is ~80% lipid, hence fat sound speed used. g-ratio = axon diameter / total fiber diameter.

Source: Huang et al., Science 1991 (first OCT paper); Grover et al., Ophthalmology 2009. Foveal center: 150-200 um; perifoveal: 300-350 um.
Notes: Measured by OCT. Thins in glaucoma and macular degeneration.

Dimension_Description: Total retinal thickness at perifoveal region
Source: Grover et al., Ophthalmology 2009. Perifoveal: 300-350 um (mean ~330 um).
Notes: Thicker than fovea due to presence of inner retinal layers.

Dimension_Description: Diameter of optic nerve behind the globe
Source: Ozgen & Ariyurek, Eur J Radiol 1998;26:274-277; Geeraerts et al., Intensive Care Med 2008. Optic nerve 3-5 mm; optic nerve sheath diameter (ONSD) 5-6 mm.
Notes: ONSD >5 mm measured by ultrasound correlates with raised intracranial pressure.

Dimension_Description: Thickness of hyaline cartilage on medial femoral condyle
Source: Adam et al., Skeletal Radiol 1998;27:205-210; Eckstein et al., Osteoarthritis Cartilage 2001;9:S2-S8. Femoral condyle: 2.0-2.5 mm; patella: 3-4 mm; tibial plateau: 1.5-2.0 mm.
Notes: Measured by MRI. Thins in osteoarthritis.

Source: Frobin et al., Spine 1997;22:990-1001; Amonoo-Kuofi, Acta Anat 1991. Lumbar disc height: 7-14 mm (mean ~10 mm at L4-L5). Cervical: 3-6 mm.
Notes: Decreases with aging and degeneration. Measured on lateral radiograph or MRI.

Dimension_Description: AP thickness at midpoint, 2-6 cm above insertion
Source: Koivunen-Niemela & Parkkola, Acta Radiol 1995;36:30-32; Fredberg et al., Clin J Sport Med 2008. Normal AP thickness 4.5-6.5 mm (mean ~5.5 mm). >6.6 mm = tendinopathy.
Notes: Measured by ultrasound. Thickest tendon in the body.

Dimension_Description: Cortical bone thickness at femoral mid-shaft
Source: Treece et al., Med Image Anal 2010;14:276-290; Poole et al., Bone 2010. Femoral midshaft cortex: 5-9 mm (mean ~7 mm). Thins in osteoporosis.
Notes: Cortical thickness is an independent fracture risk predictor.

Dimension_Description: Full-thickness parietal calvarium (outer table + diploe + inner table)
Source: Lynnerup et al., Forensic Sci Int 2005;154:190-195; Mahinda & Murty, Forensic Sci Int 2009;203:e1-e5. Parietal: 5-8 mm mean; frontal: 6-8 mm; occipital: 7-11 mm.
Notes: Varies with sex, age, region. Frontal bone thickest at glabella. Temporal bone thinnest (~2-3 mm).

Dimension_Description: Anteroposterior diameter of one thyroid lobe
Source: Hegedus et al., Endocr Rev 2003;24:897-934. Normal lobe: length 40-60 mm, AP 13-20 mm (mean ~18 mm), width 15-20 mm. Volume per lobe 6-8 mL.
Notes: Measured by ultrasound. Varies with iodine status and sex.

Source: Vincent et al., AJR 1994. Normal adrenal limb thickness <5-6 mm by CT; body thickness <10 mm. Total gland length ~30-50 mm.
Notes: Right adrenal pyramidal/triangular; left adrenal crescentic.

Source: Elster AD, Radiology 1993;187:149-153; Tsunoda et al., AJNR 1997. Height: 3-8 mm (mean ~6 mm); may be up to 10-12 mm in puberty or pregnancy. Width ~10-12 mm, AP ~8 mm.
Notes: Sits in sella turcica. Enlarges during puberty and pregnancy.

Dimension_Description: Diameter of a typical pancreatic islet
Source: Ionescu-Tirgoviste et al., J Diabetes Res 2015; Brissova et al., J Histochem Cytochem 2005. Range 50-500 um; mean ~100-200 um (150 um typical). About 1-2% of pancreatic mass.
Notes: ~1 million islets per pancreas. Contain alpha, beta, delta, PP cells.

Source: Beland et al., AJR 2010;195:W146-W149. Normal cortical thickness 7-12 mm (mean ~10 mm) by US. <7 mm suggests chronic kidney disease.
Notes: Thins with age and in CKD. Measured from capsule to base of medullary pyramid.

Dimension_Description: Detrusor muscle wall thickness when distended
Source: Oelke et al., Eur Urol 2007;51:1522-1533. Normal bladder wall 2-4 mm when distended (>250 mL). Up to 5 mm when partially filled.
Notes: >5 mm when distended suggests bladder outlet obstruction or neurogenic bladder.

Source: Merz et al., Ultrasound Med Biol 1996. Total uterine AP diameter ~40-50 mm; myometrial thickness ~12-18 mm in reproductive age. Thins postmenopause.
Notes: Varies with parity, menstrual phase, and age.

Source: Hricak et al., Radiology 1987;169:389-392; Grossfeld et al., Prostate Cancer Prostatic Dis 1998. Normal: transverse 40-50 mm, AP 25-30 mm, craniocaudal 30-40 mm. Volume <30 mL.
Notes: Enlarges with BPH. Volume estimated by prolate ellipsoid formula.

Dimension_Description: Short-axis diameter of normal lymph node
Source: Dorfman et al., AJR 1991;157:137-142; Ying & Ahuja, Cancer Imaging 2003. Normal nodes generally <10 mm short axis. Typical 5-10 mm. Jugulodigastric nodes normal up to 15 mm.
Notes: >10 mm short axis = potentially pathologic (varies by location).

Source: Niederau et al., Radiology 1983;149:781-784; Lamb et al., Abdom Radiol 2002. Normal spleen: length 100-120 mm, thickness 30-50 mm (mean ~40 mm), width 60-70 mm.
Notes: Splenic index >480 cm3 or length >13 cm = splenomegaly.

Source: Baron et al., AJR 1982;138:491-498; Francis et al., Radiology 1985. Adult thymus: thickness <13 mm (mean ~7-10 mm). Involutes with age, largely replaced by fat after age 25-30.
Notes: In adults, largely fatty. In children, thymus is a prominent anterior mediastinal structure.

Source: Brodsky L, Arch Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 1989. Grading: 0 = within fossa; 1+ to 4+ = 25% to 100% of oropharynx. Typical diameter 15-25 mm.
Notes: Hypertrophic in children, involutes with age.

Source: Doughty & Zaman, Cornea 2000;19:715-723 (meta-analysis of 300+ studies). Mean CCT = 535 um (range 500-570 um). Also: Aghaian et al., Cornea 2004.
Notes: Thinnest centrally, thickens peripherally (~670 um). Affects IOP measurement accuracy.

Source: Dubbelman et al., Vision Res 2001;41:1867-1877. Young adult: ~3.5-4.0 mm unaccommodated. Increases with age: ~4.5-5.0 mm by age 70. Changes ~0.024 mm/year.
Notes: Thickens with accommodation and aging. Biconvex lens; curvature changes for focusing.

Source: Kuypers et al., JARO 2006;7:49-63; Lim DJ, Acta Otolaryngol 1968;66:181-198. Pars tensa: 50-120 um (mean ~100 um). Three layers: cutaneous, fibrous, mucosal.
Notes: Pars flaccida is thinner (~40-80 um). Vibrates in response to sound pressure.

Dimension_Description: Maximum enamel thickness over molar cusp tip
Source: Kono et al., Am J Phys Anthropol 2002;118:23-31; Schwartz & Dean, J Hum Evol 2005. Molar cusps: 2.0-3.0 mm; average ~2.5 mm. Lateral enamel: ~1.0-1.5 mm. Sound speed in enamel similar to cortical bone (~3000-6000 m/s, using ~3500).
Notes: Hardest substance in the human body (~96% mineral). Does not regenerate once lost.

Source: De Berker et al., J Cosmet Dermatol 2007;6:73-81. Fingernail: 0.4-0.65 mm (mean ~0.5 mm); toenail: 0.7-1.65 mm (mean ~1.0 mm).
Notes: Composed of hard keratin. Grows ~3 mm/month (fingernail).

Dimension_Description: Diameter of a terminal scalp hair shaft
Source: Otsuka & Nemoto, Dermatology 1988;176:1-5; Saitoh et al., J Invest Dermatol 1970. Range: 50-100 um; mean ~70 um for Caucasian hair. Asian hair thicker (~80-100 um); fine hair ~40-50 um.
Notes: Varies by ethnicity, age, body site. Medulla, cortex, and cuticle layers.
